perturbation.original_text: NEITHER PARTY SHALL HAVE ANY LIABILITY HEREUNDER FOR ANY INDIRECT, SPECIAL, PUNITIVE OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, LOSS OF PROFIT OR BUSINESS OPPORTUNITIES, WHETHER OR NOT THE PARTY WAS ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH. EXCEPT AS EXPRESSLY SET FORTH HEREIN, NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY, AND EACH PARTY HEREBY SPECIFICALLY DISCLAIMS ANY REPRESENTATIONS OR WARRANTIES, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, REGARDING THE SERVICES CONTEMPLATED BY THIS AGREEMENT, INCLUDING ANY IMPLIED WARRANTY OF MERCHANTABILITY OR FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND IMPLIED WARRANTIES AFJSING FROM COURSE OF DEALING OR COURSE OF PERFORMANCE.
perturbation.changed_text: NEITHER PARTY SHALL HAVE ANY LIABILITY HEREUNDER FOR ANY INDIRECT, SPECIAL, PUNITIVE OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, LOSS OF PROFIT OR BUSINESS OPPORTUNITIES, WHETHER OR NOT THE PARTY WAS ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH. EXCEPT AS EXPRESSLY SET FORTH HEREIN, NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY, AND EACH PARTY HEREBY SPECIFICALLY DISCLAIMS ANY REPRESENTATIONS OR WARRANTIES, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, REGARDING THE SERVICES CONTEMPLATED BY THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'INCLUDING ANY IMPLIED WARRANTY OF MERCHANTABILITY OR FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND IMPLIED WARRANTIES AFJSING FROM COURSE OF DEALING OR COURSE OF PERFORMANCE' the clause now attempts to disclaim implied warranties which may be illegal depending on the nature of services provided and governing state law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-316 - Exclusion or Modification of Warranties
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-316 sets specific requirements for disclaiming implied warranties, particularly the warranty of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. Removing the explicit mention of these warranties, while still attempting a general disclaimer, may not be sufficient under the UCC, especially if the disclaimer is not conspicuous or doesn't use specific language required by the statute. This creates a legal risk as it makes the disclaimer potentially unenforceable, leaving the parties open to liability for breaches of implied warranties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes specific disclaimers of implied warranties, potentially violating UCC § 2-316. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that excluding or modifying the implied warranty of merchantability requires mentioning 'merchantability'. By removing the phrase 'INCLUDING ANY IMPLIED WARRANTY OF MERCHANTABILITY OR FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND IMPLIED WARRANTIES AFJSING FROM COURSE OF DEALING OR COURSE OF PERFORMANCE,' the contract no longer explicitly disclaims these warranties, creating a legal risk as the disclaimer might be unenforceable. This omission directly contradicts the requirement for explicit mention as stated in the UCC.

perturbation.original_text: iVillage agrees to indemnify, defend and hold harmless FMM and its client Ford Motor Company and their respective parents, subsidiaries, affiliates, successors and assigns from any and all third party losses, liabilities, damages, actions, claims, expenses and costs (including reasonable attorneys' fees) which result or arise out of or in connection with the breach of this 5 Agreement by iVillage or which result or arise out of or in connection with any material supplied by iVillage pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: iVillage agrees to indemnify FMM from any and all third party losses, liabilities, damages, actions, claims, expenses and costs which result or arise out of or in connection with the breach of this 5 Agreement by iVillage.
perturbation.explanation: By severely limiting the scope of indemnification, specifically removing 'defend and hold harmless,' as well as 'its client Ford Motor Company and their respective parents, subsidiaries, affiliates, successors and assigns' and 'or which result or arise out of or in connection with any material supplied by iVillage pursuant to this Agreement,' iVillage weakens its protection of Ford, potentially violating standard business practice and increasing legal risk by failing to cover standard indemnification scenarios.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York General Obligations Law § 5-322.1 Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the statute primarily addresses construction contracts, the principle of ensuring responsibility for negligence is broadly applicable. By significantly weakening the indemnification clause, iVillage potentially exposes Ford (the indemnitee) to liabilities arising from iVillage's negligence or the material they supply without adequate protection, potentially violating the underlying principles of fairness and responsibility reflected in NY GOL § 5-322.1. The change omits the duty to defend and hold harmless, crucial aspects that ensure full protection in legal claims.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-322.1 Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-322.1. Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens iVillage's indemnification obligations to Ford. The original text included 'defend and hold harmless', 'its client Ford Motor Company and their respective parents, subsidiaries, affiliates, successors and assigns' and 'or which result or arise out of or in connection with any material supplied by iVillage pursuant to this Agreement.' These were removed in the changed text. The Law Explanation indicates that omitting the duty to defend and hold harmless, which are crucial aspects that ensure full protection in legal claims, potentially violates the principles of fairness. NY GOL § 5-322.1, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, addresses agreements exempting parties from liability for negligence, indicating a concern for ensuring responsibility. While the statute primarily addresses construction contracts, the underlying principle of ensuring responsibility for negligence is broadly applicable, as mentioned in the Law Explanation. The omission of key protections in the indemnification clause could expose Ford to liabilities arising from iVillage's negligence without adequate protection, thus creating a legal contradiction because it undermines established expectations of proper indemnification.

perturbation.original_text: All notices, requests, demands, payments and other communications which are required or may be given under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given if delivered personally, telecopied or sent by nationally recognized overnight carrier, or mailed by certified mail, postage prepaid, return receipt requested, as follows: If to FMM: Ford Motor Media 500 Woodward Avenue Detroit, Michigan 48226-3428 Attention: Carol Wright Tel: (313) 964-2954 Fax: (313) 964-2315 If to iVillage: iVillage, Inc. 170 Fifth Avenue New York, New York 10010 Attention: Vice President Business/Legal Affairs Tel: (212) 206-3106 Fax: (212) 604-9133
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, requests, demands, payments and other communications which are required or may be given under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given if delivered by mail as follows: If to FMM: Ford Motor Media 500 Woodward Avenue Detroit, Michigan 48226-3428 If to iVillage: iVillage, Inc. 170 Fifth Avenue New York, New York 10010
perturbation.explanation: By restricting the delivery methods to only 'mail' and removing specific contact persons, phone numbers and fax numbers, this modification introduces ambiguity and increased risk of delayed or undelivered notices. This conflicts with the principle of providing reasonable means for communication as implied in contract law, and the established best practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 2103. Service of papers
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 2103 outlines the methods for serving papers in legal actions, including mail, electronic means, and personal delivery. While this section directly applies to court proceedings, the underlying principle is that service (or notice) must be reasonably calculated to reach the intended recipient. By restricting notice to 'mail' only and removing specific contact information, the modified contract creates a significant risk that critical notices (e.g., of breach, termination) will be delayed or lost, potentially leading to legal disputes over proper notice. Removing the attention line, phone and fax only exacerbates the risk.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The changed text restricts the acceptable methods of communication to 'mail', removing options like personal delivery, telecopy, and overnight carrier, as well as specific contact details. This omission increases the risk that critical notices will be delayed or lost. Although the provided snippets do not offer direct evidence, the Law Explanation indicates that service (or notice) must be reasonably calculated to reach the intended recipient. The original text provided several ways to contact, whereas the changed text severely restricts them, undermining the principle of reasonable notice.
